summary:
The Judet view is primarily used to evaluate fractures involving the scaphoid bone in the wrist. This makes it clinically significant among options related to hand or forearm injuries where such specific bony alignment assessment is required.

Okay, I need to summarize Agent2’s reasoning about why a Judet view of an X-ray is performed — focusing strictly on its primary use based on clinical relevance without adding external details beyond what was provided by them.

First sentence should state clearly that the Judet view evaluates fratured scaphoid bones—this comes directly from their explanation as being “the typical site” assessed using this radiographic technique.

Second sentence must emphasize how this fits into broader diagnostic needs—for example, assessing stability or misalignment during injury evaluation within upper limb trauma cases specifically tied to wrists rather than shoulders or arms mentioned elsewhere.

I will keep both under two full sentences; avoid mentioning any other structures not linked explicitly through judgmental logic presented here—and ensure all content stems solely from Agent2’s own stated rationale.
